subject: Math

lesson.story_mission: Help the Measurement Makers label classroom objects with accurate lengths.
lesson.mini_lesson: Measure length by lining up one end at zero, reading the endpoint, and naming the unit. Estimate when an exact ruler is not needed.
lesson.objective: Measure and estimate lengths using inches and centimeters, then compare length differences.
lesson.concept_explanation: A length is the distance from one end of an object to the other. The unit tells what size marks you counted.
lesson.common_misconception: Learners may start at the edge of the ruler instead of zero or mix inches and centimeters.
lesson.real_world_connection: Measuring helps choose materials for crafts, classroom objects, and building projects.
lesson.lesson_summary: Start at zero, count same-size units, record the unit, and subtract to compare lengths.
hints: Line up the object at 0.
Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

worked_examples.text: A pencil from 0 to 5 inches is 5 inches long.
A string from 3 cm to 10 cm is 7 cm long because 10 - 3 = 7.
If one ribbon is 9 inches and another is 5 inches, the difference is 4 inches.

hint_ladder: Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

misconception_bank.starts_not_at_zero: Learner may count from the ruler edge or first mark instead of zero.
misconception_bank.unit_confusion: Learner may give inches when the ruler is centimeters, or centimeters when it is inches.
misconception_bank.estimate_error: Learner may choose an unreasonable length for a familiar object.
misconception_bank.length_difference_confusion: Learner may report one object length instead of subtracting to find the difference.

questions.prompt: A pencil starts at 0 and ends at 5 inches. How long is the pencil?
hints: Focus: Find where the pencil begins and ends on the inches ruler.
Strategy: Because it begins at zero, read the ending mark as its length. In the a pencil starts at 0 and ends at 5 inches situation.
Verify: Check that 0 plus your measured length reaches the ending mark at 5. In the a pencil starts at 0 and ends at 5 inches situation.

feedback.correct: Your measurement reasoning is on track.
feedback.incorrect: Recheck the visual, the unit, and each measurement step.

questions.explanation: The pencil begins at 0, so the endpoint 5 names its length: 5 inches.

questions.prompt: A red ribbon is 9 inches. A blue ribbon is 5 inches. How many inches longer is the red ribbon?
hints: Focus: Identify the longer measurement (9) and the shorter measurement (5) in inches.
Strategy: Subtract the shorter length from the longer length: 9 − 5. In the a red ribbon is 9 inches situation.
Verify: Add your difference to 5; it should rebuild the longer length, 9. In the a red ribbon is 9 inches situation.

feedback.correct: Your measurement reasoning is on track.
feedback.incorrect: Recheck the visual, the unit, and each measurement step.

questions.explanation: A length difference tells how much farther the longer object extends. 9 − 5 = 4, and 5 + 4 = 9, so the difference is 4 inches.

questions.prompt: A photo frame edge starts at 2 inches and ends at 11 inches. How long is the edge?
hints: Focus: Locate both endpoints of the photo frame edge; it does not start at zero.
Strategy: Find the interval by subtracting the start, 2, from the end, 11. In the a photo frame edge starts at 2 inches and ends at 11 inches situation.
Verify: Add your result to 2 and confirm that you land on 11. In the a photo frame edge starts at 2 inches and ends at 11 inches situation.

questions.prompt: A model bridge is 19 inches long. A model boat is 11 inches long. How many inches longer is the bridge?
hints: Focus: Compare the 19-inche and 11-inche lengths.
Strategy: Subtract the smaller measurement, 11, from the larger, 19. In the a model bridge is 19 inches long situation.
Verify: Add 8 to 11 to check that it equals 19. In the a model bridge is 19 inches long situation.
